These results demonstrate compact, low-latency computational infrared imaging for real-time object detection on resource-constrained platforms.

AI.tldr: PDI-Net is a low-latency framework that integrates infrared image reconstruction and object detection by embedding optical priors (field-dependent PSF) via a PALS-Bridge module, using a semi-U-Net encoder that shares features directly with a YOLO detector to avoid full image reconstruction. It reduces inference time by 84% and improves mAP by 5% on the M3FD benchmark, deployed on a single-lens camera with 50% weight reduction.
AI.motivation: Existing deep-learning pipelines for computational infrared imaging combine reconstruction and detection but suffer from high inference latency. Most acceleration strategies compress the reconstruction network while ignoring physical optical priors, creating an accuracy-speed trade-off.
AI.method: PDI-Net uses a supervised U-Net during training, but during inference a semi-U-Net encoder shares features directly with a YOLO-based detector (bypassing full reconstruction). A physics-aware large-small bridge (PALS-Bridge) uses field-dependent point spread function (PSF) priors to adaptively modulate multiscale convolutional branches, bridging fidelity-oriented reconstruction features with detection-oriented semantics. A physics-informed optical degradation simulation pipeline is also developed.
AI.result: Deployed on a single-lens infrared camera (~50% weight reduction vs. multi-lens designs). On M3FD benchmark under low-SNR conditions: inference time reduced by 84.06% compared to Rec+Det with pruning, while mAP@0.5:0.95 improved by 5.07%.
AI.conclusion: PDI-Net enables compact, low-latency computational infrared imaging for real-time object detection on resource-constrained platforms, successfully overcoming the accuracy-speed trade-off by embedding optical priors into the learning process.

title: Foresee-to-Ground: From Predictive Temporal Perception to Evidence-Driven Reasoning for Video Temporal Grounding
summary: Current Video-LLM approaches for Video Temporal Grounding (VTG) typically rely on direct timestamp generation from an unstructured visual-token stream, often leading to brittle numerics and inconsistent boundaries. To address this, we propose Foresee-to-Ground (F2G), a framework that reformulates VTG as a verifiable Identify-then-Measure problem. F2G integrates Predictive Temporal Perception with Evidence-Driven Reasoning: it learns boundary-sensitive temporal representations to build a video-wide evidence pool of candidate event segments, and exposes these segments to the LLM as citable evidence units that bind boundary prediction to explicit event hypotheses. By decoupling event identification from precise boundary measurement, F2G stabilizes grounding and makes predictions verifiable. Extensive experiments demonstrate that F2G consistently improves grounding accuracy across diverse benchmarks, transfers robustly across different Video-LLM backbones, and preserves general video understanding capabilities.

AI.tldr: F2G reformulates Video Temporal Grounding as a verifiable Identify-then-Measure process by integrating Predictive Temporal Perception with Evidence-Driven Reasoning, decoupling event identification from boundary measurement to improve grounding accuracy and robustness.
AI.motivation: Current Video-LLM methods for VTG rely on direct timestamp generation from unstructured visual-token streams, which produces brittle numerics and inconsistent boundaries.
AI.method: F2G framework: (1) Predictive Temporal Perception learns boundary-sensitive temporal representations to build a video-wide evidence pool of candidate event segments; (2) Evidence-Driven Reasoning exposes these segments to the LLM as citable evidence units, binding boundary prediction to explicit event hypotheses; (3) Decouples event identification from precise boundary measurement.
AI.result: F2G consistently improves grounding accuracy across diverse benchmarks, transfers robustly across different Video-LLM backbones, and preserves general video understanding capabilities.
AI.conclusion: By reformulating VTG as a verifiable Identify-then-Measure problem, F2G stabilizes grounding and makes predictions more verifiable and accurate.

title: Video as Natural Augmentation: Towards Unified AI-Generated Image and Video Detection
summary: AI-generated content (AIGC) is rapidly improving, creating an urgent need for detectors that generalize across data sources, deployment pipelines, and visual modalities. A strongly generalizable detector should remain robust under distributional variations. However, we identify a consistent failure mode: SOTA AI-generated image detectors often collapse when applied to frames extracted from videos. Through systematic analysis, we show that this cross-modal gap arises from both entangled synthesis-agnostic video processing shifts, including color conversion, codec compression, resizing, and blur, and model-specific fingerprints introduced by modern video generators. Motivated by these findings, we propose VINA (Video as Natural Augmentation), a unified AIGC detection framework that jointly trains on image and video data. VINA uses video frames as physically grounded natural augmentations and further introduces a cross-modal supervised contrastive objective to align image and video representations under a shared real/fake decision boundary. Extensive experiments on 14 image, video, and in-the-wild benchmarks show that VINA delivers bidirectional gains, improves robustness and transferability, and achieves state-of-the-art performance across nearly all evaluated settings without complex augmentation or dataset-specific tuning.

AI.tldr: VINA is a unified AIGC detection framework that jointly trains on images and videos, using video frames as natural augmentations and cross-modal contrastive learning to bridge the image-video gap, achieving SOTA across 14 benchmarks.
AI.motivation: SOTA AI-generated image detectors fail when applied to video frames due to cross-modal gaps caused by video processing shifts (color conversion, codec compression, resizing, blur) and model-specific video generator fingerprints.
AI.method: VINA jointly trains on image and video data, using video frames as physically grounded natural augmentations, and introduces a cross-modal supervised contrastive objective to align image and video representations under a shared real/fake decision boundary.
AI.result: VINA achieves bidirectional gains, improves robustness and transferability, and achieves SOTA on nearly all of 14 evaluated image, video, and in-the-wild benchmarks without complex augmentation or dataset-specific tuning.
AI.conclusion: VINA effectively bridges the cross-modal detection gap and demonstrates strong generalization across data sources, deployment pipelines, and visual modalities.

title: Interpreting and Enhancing Emotional Circuits in Large Vision-Language Models via Cross-Modal Information Flow
summary: Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) represent a significant leap towards empathetic agents, demonstrating remarkable capabilities in emotion understanding. However, the internal mechanisms governing how LVLMs translate abstract visual stimuli into coherent emotional narratives remain largely unexplored, primarily due to the scarcity of visual counterfactuals and the diffuse nature of emotional expression. In this paper, we bridge this gap by introducing a steering-vector-based causal attribution framework tailored for descriptive emotional reasoning. To this end, we construct a specialized dataset to demystify the emotional circuits underlying the three-stage ``Adapt-Aggregate-Execute'' mechanism. Crucially, we discover a functional decoupling: visual emotional cues are aggregated in middle layers via sentiment-specific attention heads, but are subsequently translated into narrative generation in deep layers through emotion-general pathways. Guided by these insights, we regulate the emotional information routing to strengthen attention flow and amplify the semantic activation to consolidate expression. Extensive experiments on the comprehensive MER-UniBench demonstrate that our methods significantly improve performance via inference-time intervention, effectively mitigating emotional hallucinations and corroborating the causal fidelity of the discovered circuits.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a steering-vector-based causal attribution framework to uncover and regulate the internal emotional reasoning mechanisms of LVLMs, revealing a functional decoupling where visual emotional cues are aggregated in middle layers via sentiment-specific attention heads and translated into narrative generation in deep layers through emotion-general pathways.
AI.motivation: The internal mechanisms governing how LVLMs translate abstract visual stimuli into coherent emotional narratives remain largely unexplored due to the scarcity of visual counterfactuals and the diffuse nature of emotional expression.
AI.method: A steering-vector-based causal attribution framework tailored for descriptive emotional reasoning, with a specialized dataset to demystify the three-stage "Adapt-Aggregate-Execute" mechanism.
AI.result: The methods significantly improve performance on MER-UniBench via inference-time intervention, effectively mitigating emotional hallucinations and corroborating the causal fidelity of the discovered circuits.
AI.conclusion: Emotional processing in LVLMs exhibits functional decoupling: sentiment-specific attention heads in middle layers aggregate cues, while emotion-general pathways in deep layers translate them into narrative generation.

title: RiT: Vanilla Diffusion Transformers Suffice in Representation Space
summary: Flow matching with $x$-prediction -- regressing the clean data point rather than the ambient velocity -- is known to exploit low-dimensional manifold structure effectively in pixel space \cite{li2025back}. We ask whether a pretrained representation space, while containing a low-dimensional data manifold of comparable intrinsic dimensionality, offers a distribution more favorable for flow-matching learning. Comparing pixel, SD-VAE, and DINOv2 features along four geometric axes, we find that pixel and DINOv2 share nearly identical intrinsic dimensionalities (both $\hat{d}\!\approx\!33$) yet DINOv2 exhibits $7.3\times$ higher effective rank, $35\times$ better covariance conditioning, $11.5\times$ lower excess kurtosis, and $1.7\times$ lower on-manifold interpolation error; SD-VAE latents are consistently intermediate, indicating that the advantage stems from representation-learning objectives rather than mere compression. These statistical properties render the flow-matching regression well-conditioned and remove the need for the specialized prediction heads or Riemannian transport used by prior DINOv2 diffusion methods. We propose the \emph{Representation Image Transformer} (RiT): a vanilla Diffusion Transformer trained by $x$-prediction on frozen DINOv2 features, augmented only by a dimension-aware noise schedule and joint \texttt{[CLS]}-patch modeling. On ImageNet $256{\times}256$, RiT attains FID 1.45 without guidance and 1.14 with classifier-free guidance, outperforming DiT$^\text{DH}$-XL with $19\%$ fewer parameters (676M vs.\ 839M). The resulting ODE is efficiently solvable at coarse discretizations: with classifier-free guidance, $5$ Heun steps already reach FID 2.0 and $10$ steps reach 1.25, without distillation or consistency training. Code at https://github.com/lezhang7/RiT.

AI.tldr: RiT is a vanilla Diffusion Transformer trained with x-prediction on frozen DINOv2 features, achieving strong FID scores (1.45 unguided, 1.14 with CFG) on ImageNet 256×256 with fewer parameters than DiT-XL, while enabling efficient ODE solving with as few as 5-10 steps.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether pretrained representation spaces (like DINOv2) offer more favorable statistical properties for flow-matching learning compared to pixel space, potentially eliminating the need for specialized architectures or Riemannian transport.
AI.method: Comparative analysis of pixel, SD-VAE, and DINOv2 features along four geometric axes (intrinsic dimensionality, effective rank, covariance conditioning, excess kurtosis, on-manifold interpolation error). Proposes RiT: a vanilla Diffusion Transformer with x-prediction on frozen DINOv2 features, dimension-aware noise schedule, and joint [CLS]-patch modeling.
AI.result: DINOv2 vs pixel: 7.3× higher effective rank, 35× better covariance conditioning, 11.5× lower excess kurtosis, 1.7× lower on-manifold interpolation error. RiT achieves FID 1.45 (no guidance) and 1.14 (CFG) on ImageNet 256×256, outperforming DiT-XL with 19% fewer parameters. With CFG, 5 Heun steps reach FID 2.0 and 10 steps reach 1.25.
AI.conclusion: Pretrained representation spaces like DINOv2 provide significantly better-conditioned distributions for flow-matching regression. The RiT architecture demonstrates that simple, generic architectures can achieve state-of-the-art generation when combined with frozen DINOv2 features, with efficient few-step sampling.

title: Learning Spatiotemporal Sensitivity in Video LLMs via Counterfactual Reinforcement Learning
summary: Video large language models (Video LLMs) achieve strong benchmark accuracy, yet often answer video questions through shortcuts such as single-frame cues and language priors rather than by tracking spatiotemporal dynamics. This issue is exacerbated in RL post-training, where correctness-only rewards can further reinforce shortcut policies that obtain high reward without tracking video dynamics. We address this by asking a controlled counterfactual question: if the visual world changed while the question remained fixed, should the answer change or stay the same? Based on this view, we propose \textbf{Counterfactual Relational Policy Optimization (CRPO)}, a dual-branch RL framework for improving \emph{spatiotemporal sensitivity}. CRPO constructs counterfactual videos through horizontal flips and temporal reversals, trains on both original and counterfactual branches, and introduces a \textbf{Counterfactual Relation Reward (CRR)} between their answers. CRR encourages answers to change for dynamic questions and remain unchanged for static questions. This cross-branch constraint makes it difficult for shortcut policies to be consistently rewarded across both branches. To evaluate this property, we introduce \textbf{DyBench}, a paired counterfactual video benchmark with 3,014 videos covering reversible dynamics, moving direction, and event sequence, together with a strict pair-accuracy metric that prevents fixed-answer shortcuts from inflating scores. Experiments show that CRPO outperforms prior RL methods on spatiotemporal-sensitive evaluations while maintaining competitive general video performance. On Qwen3-VL-8B, CRPO improves DyBench P-Acc by +7.7 and TimeBlind I-Acc by +8.2 over the base model, indicating improved spatiotemporal sensitivity rather than stronger reliance on static shortcuts. The project website can be found at https://ddz16.github.io/crpo.github.io/ .

AI.tldr: CRPO is a dual-branch RL framework that uses counterfactual videos and a Counterfactual Relation Reward to improve spatiotemporal sensitivity in Video LLMs, outperforming prior RL methods on dynamic reasoning benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Video LLMs often rely on shortcuts (single-frame cues, language priors) rather than tracking spatiotemporal dynamics, and standard RL post-training with correctness-only rewards reinforces these shortcut policies.
AI.method: CRPO uses a dual-branch RL framework: it constructs counterfactual videos (horizontal flips, temporal reversals), trains on both original and counterfactual branches, and introduces a Counterfactual Relation Reward (CRR) that encourages answers to change for dynamic questions and stay fixed for static questions.
AI.result: On Qwen3-VL-8B, CRPO improves DyBench P-Acc by +7.7 and TimeBlind I-Acc by +8.2 over the base model. It outperforms prior RL methods on spatiotemporal-sensitive evaluations while maintaining competitive general video performance.
AI.conclusion: CRPO effectively improves spatiotemporal sensitivity in Video LLMs by using counterfactual relational constraints, preventing shortcut policies from being rewarded across both original and manipulated video branches.

title: Virtual 3D H&E Staining from Phase-contrast Back-illumination Interference Tomography
summary: Three-dimensional (3D) histopathology of unprocessed tissues has the potential to transform disease management by enabling volumetric characterization of tissue microarchitecture and in-vivo assessment. Back-illumination Interference Tomography (BIT) is a new phase microscopy technology that provides rapid, non-destructive volumetric imaging of unprocessed tissues. However, translating BIT volumes into clinically interpretable H&E images remains challenging, particularly due to shift-variant contrast and the absence of quantitative validation benchmarks. We introduce HistoBIT3D, the first voxel-wise paired BIT and fluorescence-labeled nuclei dataset, enabling quantitative evaluation of structural preservation in unsupervised virtual staining against ground-truth nuclear distributions. Using this dataset, we present a novel virtual staining framework that translates BIT volumes with shift-variant contrast into realistic H&E volumes by leveraging bidirectional multiscale content consistency and cross-domain style reuse to enhance structural fidelity and perceptual realism. Our method achieves state-of-the-art realism metrics while significantly improving 3D nuclei segmentation accuracy and boundary preservation under zero-shot Cellpose evaluation. Together, these contributions establish a quantitatively validated, structurally faithful, and scalable pipeline for 3D virtual H&E staining, advancing the paradigm of slide-free, volumetric computational histopathology. Our data and code are available at: https://github.com/aasong113/HistoBIT3D_VirtualStaining.

AI.tldr: HistoBIT3D introduces the first voxel-wise paired BIT and fluorescence nuclei dataset and a novel virtual staining framework that translates BIT volumes into realistic 3D H&E images, achieving state-of-the-art realism and improved nuclei segmentation accuracy.
AI.motivation: To enable clinically interpretable H&E-like images from BIT phase microscopy volumes, addressing challenges of shift-variant contrast and lack of quantitative validation benchmarks in 3D virtual staining.
AI.method: A virtual staining framework using bidirectional multiscale content consistency and cross-domain style reuse, trained on the first voxel-wise paired BIT and fluorescence-labeled nuclei dataset (HistoBIT3D).
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art realism metrics, significantly improves 3D nuclei segmentation accuracy and boundary preservation under zero-shot Cellpose evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Establishes a quantitatively validated, structurally faithful, and scalable pipeline for 3D virtual H&E staining, advancing slide-free volumetric computational histopathology.

title: ConvNeXt-FD: A Fractal-Based Deep Model for Robust Biomedical Image Segmentation
summary: Biomedical image segmentation is a critical task in medical diagnosis and treatment planning, enabling precise delineation of anatomical structures and pathological regions. Despite significant advancements, challenges persist due to the inherent variability, noise, and complex morphology present in diverse medical imaging modalities. This paper introduces ConvNeXt-FD, a novel deep learning architecture for robust biomedical image segmentation, built upon a U-Net-like encoder-decoder framework leveraging the powerful ConvNeXt backbone. Our approach integrates a hybrid loss function combining the Dice coefficient with a boundary-aware regularization term inspired by a differentiable formulation of Fractal Dimension, designed to enhance the model's sensitivity to object boundaries and shape fidelity. We rigorously evaluate ConvNeXt-FD across six distinct biomedical datasets: BUSI (Breast Ultrasound Images), DDTI (Thyroid Ultrasound Images), FluoCells (Fluorescent Cell Images), IDRiD (Diabetic Retinopathy Images for Optic Disc Segmentation), ISIC2018 (Skin Lesion Images), and MoNuSeg (Nuclei Segmentation). Experimental results demonstrate that ConvNeXt-FD, particularly when initialized with ImageNet pre-trained weights, achieves competitive and often superior performance compared to existing state-of-the-art methods across various metrics, including Dice, Jaccard, Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, and False Positive Rate. The integration of ConvNeXt as a strong encoder, coupled with the boundary-aware regularization, proves effective in capturing both high-level semantic features and fine-grained boundary details, leading to more accurate and reliable segmentations in challenging biomedical contexts.

AI.tldr: ConvNeXt-FD is a U-Net-like deep learning architecture with a ConvNeXt backbone and a hybrid loss function incorporating fractal dimension-based boundary regularization. It achieves competitive segmentation performance across six diverse biomedical datasets.
AI.motivation: Biomedical image segmentation faces challenges from variability, noise, and complex morphology in medical images. Existing methods struggle to simultaneously capture high-level semantics and fine boundary details.
AI.method: ConvNeXt-FD uses a U-Net encoder-decoder with a ConvNeXt backbone and a hybrid loss combining Dice coefficient with a differentiable fractal dimension-based boundary-aware regularization term. ImageNet pre-training is leveraged.
AI.result: Evaluated on BUSI, DDTI, FluoCells, IDRiD, ISIC2018, and MoNuSeg, ConvNeXt-FD achieves competitive/superior performance across Dice, Jaccard, Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, and FPR metrics compared to state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: The ConvNeXt backbone with boundary-aware fractal dimension regularization effectively enhances both semantic feature extraction and boundary detail preservation, yielding more accurate segmentations in challenging biomedical contexts.

title: Rethinking Token Reduction for Diffusion Models via Output-Similarity-Awareness
summary:
Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) achieve superior image generation quality but suffer from quadratic computational complexity relative to token count. While various token reduction (TR) methods have been proposed to mitigate this cost, they overlook the primary objective of generative models: minimizing recovery error, which requires reflecting output token similarity. They rely solely on input token similarity inherited from reduction-only ViT paradigms, leading to a fundamental misalignment with this objective.
  To bridge this gap, we propose DiTo, a novel TR paradigm that shifts the focus toward output-centric token reduction. Based on the observation that output token similarity is consistently preserved across adjacent timesteps, DiTo utilizes prior-step similarities as an effective proxy to establish token correspondences at a Matching timestep, which are then reused across multiple subsequent Reduction timesteps. To optimize this interleaved scheduling, we propose Pair Match Ratio (PMR)-guided Interval Scheduling to determine the optimal matching frequency. Furthermore, to mitigate localized approximation errors and resulting blocking artifacts caused by repeated reuse, we propose Frequency-aware Token Matching by incorporating a selection-frequency penalty. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DiTo consistently outperforms existing TR methods with 1.6-3.9 dB higher PSNR at comparable speedups, achieving a superior Pareto frontier.

AI.tldr: DiTo introduces output-centric token reduction for Diffusion Transformers, using prior-step output token similarity as a proxy to reduce computation, with PMR-guided scheduling and frequency-aware matching to achieve superior quality-efficiency trade-offs (1.6-3.9 dB PSNR improvement).
AI.motivation: Existing token reduction methods for Diffusion Transformers rely on input token similarity inherited from ViT paradigms, which misaligns with the primary objective of generative models—minimizing recovery error. This oversight causes suboptimal performance.
AI.method: DiTo proposes (1) output-centric token reduction using prior-step output token similarity as a proxy, (2) PMR-guided Interval Scheduling to determine optimal matching frequency, and (3) Frequency-aware Token Matching with a selection-frequency penalty to mitigate blocking artifacts from repeated reuse.
AI.result: DiTo consistently outperforms existing TR methods with 1.6-3.9 dB higher PSNR at comparable speedups, achieving a superior Pareto frontier in the quality-efficiency trade-off.
AI.conclusion: Shifting from input-centric to output-centric token reduction better aligns with generative objectives and yields significant quality improvements, demonstrating the importance of objective-aware token reduction design.

title: PointLLM-R: Enhancing 3D Point Cloud Reasoning via Chain-of-Thought
summary: Understanding 3D point clouds through language remains a fundamental challenge in computer graphics and visual computing, due to the irregular structure of point cloud data and the lack of explicit reasoning in existing 3D multimodal models. While Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning has shown strong effectiveness in LLMs and image-based MLLMs, its extension to 3D understanding remains largely underexplored. In this paper, we propose a data-centric framework for constructing large-scale CoT supervision tailored to 3D point cloud understanding. Our framework consists of a two-stage pipeline that first refines point-text instruction data via vision-language-model-based quality evaluation and reference-guided refinement, and then synthesizes high-quality reasoning paths through Human-in-the-Loop Prompt Optimization (HiLPO). Using this approach, we build PoCoTI, a CoT-enhanced point-text instruction-following dataset containing 55K samples with explicit reasoning paths. Fine-tuning PointLLM on PoCoTI yields PointLLM-R, a reasoning-capable 3D multimodal language model. Extensive experiments on generative 3D classification and captioning demonstrate that PointLLM-R achieves state-of-the-art performance and generalizes robustly to real-world scanned point clouds and multi-turn dialogue scenarios.

AI.tldr: A data-centric framework (PoCoTI) generates 55K Chain-of-Thought reasoning samples for 3D point clouds via VLM-based quality evaluation and Human-in-the-Loop Prompt Optimization. Fine-tuning PointLLM on this dataset yields PointLLM-R, achieving SOTA on 3D classification and captioning tasks.
AI.motivation: 3D point cloud understanding with language is challenging due to irregular data structure and lack of explicit reasoning in existing 3D multimodal models. CoT reasoning is effective in LLMs/images but underexplored in 3D.
AI.method: A two-stage pipeline: (1) Refine point-text instruction data via VLM-based quality evaluation and reference-guided refinement; (2) Synthesize high-quality reasoning paths via Human-in-the-Loop Prompt Optimization (HiLPO). Builds PoCoTI dataset (55K samples) and fine-tunes PointLLM to create PointLLM-R.
AI.result: PointLLM-R achieves state-of-the-art performance on generative 3D classification and captioning, and generalizes robustly to real-world scanned point clouds and multi-turn dialogue.
AI.conclusion: Explicit CoT reasoning supervision significantly improves 3D multimodal language model performance; the proposed data-centric framework is effective for constructing large-scale reasoning datasets for 3D understanding.

title: ORBIS: Output-Guided Token Reduction with Distribution-Aware Matching for Video Diffusion Acceleration
summary:
Diffusion Transformer (DiT) has emerged as a powerful model architecture for generating high-quality images and videos. In the case of video DiT, 3D Spatio-Temporal Attention increases token length in proportion to the number of frames, sharply increasing computational cost. Token reduction methods mitigate this cost by exploiting spatial redundancy, but existing approaches rely on inaccurate similarity estimates and lightweight matching algorithms, resulting in poor matching quality and only marginal acceleration.
  To overcome these limitations, we propose ORBIS, an SW-HW co-designed accelerator for video DiT. ORBIS leverages the output activation from the previous timestep to obtain more accurate inter-token similarity, substantially improving matching quality and enabling a higher token reduction ratio. We further introduce a Distribution-Aware Token Matching (DATM) algorithm that captures global token distribution and explicitly minimizes token-pair loss for additional gains. To fully hide DATM latency, we design specialized, deeply pipelined hardware and minimize its hardware cost through quantization, occupying only 2.4% of total area with negligible accuracy loss. Extensive experiments show that ORBIS achieves about 2x higher token reduction ratio than the state-of-the-art approach, AsymRnR, while delivering up to 4.5x speedup and 79.3% energy reduction compared to an NVIDIA A100 GPU.

AI.tldr: ORBIS is an SW-HW co-designed accelerator for video Diffusion Transformers that improves token reduction through better similarity estimation and a distribution-aware matching algorithm, achieving up to 4.5x speedup and 79.3% energy savings over an A100 GPU.
AI.motivation: Video DiT models suffer from high computational cost due to 3D Spatio-Temporal Attention increasing token length with frame count. Existing token reduction methods use inaccurate similarity estimates and lightweight matching, yielding poor quality and marginal acceleration.
AI.method: ORBIS proposes (1) leveraging previous timestep output activations for more accurate inter-token similarity, (2) a Distribution-Aware Token Matching (DATM) algorithm capturing global token distribution and minimizing token-pair loss, and (3) specialized deeply pipelined hardware with quantization that hides DATM latency at only 2.4% area overhead.
AI.result: ORBIS achieves ~2x higher token reduction ratio vs. AsymRnR, up to 4.5x speedup, and 79.3% energy reduction compared to NVIDIA A100 GPU.
AI.conclusion: SW-HW co-design with better similarity estimation and distribution-aware matching can significantly accelerate video DiT inference while maintaining accuracy.

title: Diverse Yet Consistent: Context-Guided Diffusion with Energy-Based Joint Refinement for Multi-Agent Motion Prediction
summary: Deepgenerative models havebecomeapromisingapproach for human motion prediction due to their ability to capture multimodal distributions and represent diverse human be haviors. However, generating predictions that are both di verse and jointly consistent among interacting agents re mains challenging. In addition, most existing approaches are primarily evaluated using single-agent (marginal) met rics, which fail to fully reflect the joint dynamics of multi agent interactions. We propose a diffusion-based frame work that improves multi-agent motion prediction by lever aging rich contextual information from historical trajecto ries. This information is incorporated through a guidance mechanism to enhance the diversity and expressiveness of predicted motions. To further enforce interaction consis tency, we introduce an energy-based formulation that re fines the joint trajectory distribution while preserving the plausibility of individual trajectories. Extensive experi ments on four benchmark datasets demonstrate that our approach consistently outperforms existing methods. No tably, our approach substantially improves both marginal (ADE/FDE) and joint (JADE/JFDE) metrics on ETH/UCY over strong marginal baselines. Compared with prior joint prediction methods, it delivers significant gains in marginal metrics while maintaining competitive joint performance.

AI.tldr: A diffusion-based framework for multi-agent motion prediction that uses historical trajectory context via guidance and an energy-based formulation to improve both diversity and joint consistency, outperforming existing methods on benchmark datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing human motion prediction methods struggle to generate predictions that are both diverse and jointly consistent among interacting agents, and most are evaluated using single-agent metrics that fail to capture multi-agent interaction dynamics.
AI.method: The paper proposes (1) a diffusion-based framework leveraging rich historical trajectory context through a guidance mechanism for diversity, and (2) an energy-based formulation to refine joint trajectory distribution while preserving individual plausibility.
AI.result: Outperforms existing methods on four benchmark datasets (ETH/UCY), achieving significant improvements in both marginal (ADE/FDE) and joint (JADE/JFDE) metrics, with strong gains in marginal metrics over prior joint prediction methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed diffusion-based approach with guidance and energy-based refinement effectively balances diversity and joint consistency for multi-agent motion prediction.

title: FRED: A Multi-Modal Autonomous Driving Dataset for Flooded Road Environments
summary: The Flooded Road Environments Dataset (FRED) is, to our knowledge, the first multi-modal autonomous driving dataset specifically targeting the collection of data from scenarios involving water hazards on the road. The dataset contains images from a 2.3 MP FLIR Blackfly USB3 camera, 64-beam 360$^\circ$ point clouds from an Ouster OS1-64 LiDAR, and data from an iXblue ATLANS-C IMU corrected by a Geoflex RTK GNSS, from five separate locations captured both during and after flooding events. The data has been released in two formats: a KITTI-style format for easy integration with existing data tools, and the RTMaps format for direct replay of the vehicle's data capture. We provide semantic labels to enable the training and evaluation of both single-sensor and sensor-fusion methods for water hazard detection. Position and velocity, as well as data captured under dry conditions, are provided to enable the development of location-based detection methods that may incorporate maps, and to evaluate other tasks such as localisation and SLAM.

AI.tldr: FRED is the first multi-modal autonomous driving dataset focused on water hazards, containing camera, LiDAR, and IMU/GNSS data from flooded roads with semantic labels for detection, localization, and SLAM tasks.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of autonomous driving datasets specifically capturing water hazard scenarios on roads, which are critical for safe navigation in adverse conditions.
AI.method: Collected multi-modal data (2.3MP camera, 64-beam LiDAR, IMU+RTK GNSS) from five flooded locations, released in KITTI-style and RTMaps formats with semantic labels for water hazard detection.
AI.result: First multi-modal dataset targeting water hazards on roads, including both wet and dry conditions with position/velocity data for multiple tasks.
AI.conclusion: FRED enables training and evaluation of single-sensor and sensor-fusion methods for water hazard detection, as well as other tasks like localization and SLAM.

title: ForeSplat: Optimization-Aware Foresight for Feed-Forward 3D Gaussian Splatting
summary: Feed-forward 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) models offer fast single-pass reconstruction,but scaling them to match per-scene optimization quality is fundamentally hindered by the scarcity of large-scale 3D annotations.A practical compromise is predict-then-refine,where post-prediction optimization compensates for the limited capacity of the feed-forward network.However,standard feed-forward 3DGS is trained solely for zero-step rendering error,ignoring whether its output constitutes a good initialization for the downstream optimizer.We present ForeSplat,an optimization-aware training framework that equips feed-forward 3DGS models to produce initializations explicitly designed for rapid,effective refinement.By offloading part of the scene-modeling burden to the optimizer,ForeSplat substantially reduces the capacity pressure on the feed-forward model,making high-quality reconstruction feasible even with compact networks.At its core is MetaGrad,a lightweight multi-anchor meta-gradient training rule that bypasses costly higher-order differentiation through the 3DGS optimizer.MetaGrad unrolls a short inner-loop refinement trajectory,samples anchor states,and back-propagates aggregated first-order gradients to the prediction head as a surrogate optimization-aware signal.This fine-tuning adds no inference cost and enables high-quality reconstruction within seconds after a few refinement steps.We instantiate ForeSplat on diverse backbones,including AnySplat,Pi3X,and a distilled variant tailored for edge deployment.Across all tested architectures,a ForeSplat-trained initialization converges in fewer refinement steps and reaches a higher peak reconstruction quality than its vanilla counterpart,even fully converged.The framework consistently bridges the gap between amortized prediction and per-scene optimization,establishing a practical path toward lightweight,high-fidelity 3D reconstruction.

AI.tldr: ForeSplat is an optimization-aware training framework for feed-forward 3D Gaussian Splatting that uses a meta-gradient training rule (MetaGrad) to produce initializations explicitly designed for rapid refinement, bridging the gap between single-pass prediction and per-scene optimization quality without adding inference cost.
AI.motivation: Feed-forward 3DGS models struggle to match per-scene optimization quality due to scarcity of 3D annotations. The predict-then-refine paradigm is promising, but standard training ignores whether outputs are good initializations for downstream optimization.
AI.method: ForeSpat uses MetaGrad, a multi-anchor meta-gradient training rule that unrolls a short inner-loop refinement trajectory, samples anchor states, and back-propagates aggregated first-order gradients (avoiding costly higher-order differentiation) to the prediction head as an optimization-aware signal.
AI.result: Across AnySplat, Pi3X, and edge-deployed variants, ForeSplat-trained initializations converge in fewer refinement steps and reach higher peak reconstruction quality than vanilla counterparts, even fully converged. No added inference cost.
AI.conclusion: ForeSplat bridges the gap between amortized prediction and per-scene optimization, establishing a practical path toward lightweight, high-fidelity 3D reconstruction.

title: SO-Mamba: State-Ownership Mamba for Unrolled MRI Reconstruction
summary: Accelerated MRI reconstruction requires recovering missing details while preserving anatomically coherent structures across large spatial regions. State-space models such as Mamba provide efficient long-range modeling, making them attractive learned regularizers for unrolled reconstruction. However, in a data-consistency-coupled unrolled solver, different stages operate on different reconstruction iterates, where the resident carrier should preserve coherent reconstruction content across stages while stage-dependent non-resident evidence is tied to the current update. Treating these roles uniformly can place persistent resident-carrier evidence and update-dependent non-resident evidence into the same recurrent content route. We therefore propose SO-Mamba, a state-ownership Mamba regularizer that assigns reconstruction evidence within each Mamba stage to recurrent residency, state-interface access, and non-state output correction. SO-Mamba implements this ownership rule with a State-Ownership Router (SOR), which constructs a resident carrier for recurrent content and routes non-resident evidence to affine modulation of the B/C state interfaces and an output correction outlet. The resident carrier supplies the Mamba content route, while the non-resident evidence stream adapts the state interfaces and contributes through the output outlet without entering the recurrent content route. We further introduce a two-level outer-band leakage diagnostic that separates hidden-state storage from readout expression by measuring outer-band energy in the selective-scan state trajectory and the post-scan Mamba readout. Experiments on five public MRI reconstruction benchmarks spanning diverse anatomies, sampling patterns, and coil configurations show that SO-Mamba consistently improves over CNN-, Transformer-, and Mamba-based baselines with competitive computational efficiency.

AI.tldr: SO-Mamba introduces a state-ownership Mamba regularizer for accelerated MRI reconstruction that separates resident carrier evidence (recurrent content across stages) from non-resident evidence (stage-specific updates) via a State-Ownership Router, achieving state-of-the-art results across multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: In unrolled MRI reconstruction, different stages operate on different reconstruction iterates. Existing methods treat resident (persistent across stages) and non-resident (stage-dependent) evidence uniformly, placing both into the same recurrent content route, which is suboptimal.
AI.method: SO-Mamba proposes a State-Ownership Router (SOR) that separates evidence into: (1) a resident carrier for recurrent content, (2) state-interface access for non-resident evidence via affine modulation of B/C interfaces, and (3) a non-state output correction outlet. A two-level outer-band leakage diagnostic measures hidden-state vs. readout separation.
AI.result: Consistent improvements over CNN-, Transformer-, and Mamba-based baselines on five public MRI reconstruction benchmarks with diverse anatomies, sampling patterns, and coil configurations, while maintaining competitive computational efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly separating resident and non-resident evidence routes in Mamba-based regularizers improves reconstruction quality for accelerated MRI, validating the state-ownership design principle.

title: AgroVG: A Large-Scale Multi-Source Benchmark for Agricultural Visual Grounding
summary: Visual grounding, the task of localizing objects described by natural-language expressions, is a foundational capability for agricultural AI systems, enabling applications such as selective weeding, disease monitoring, and targeted harvesting. Reliable evaluation of agricultural visual grounding remains challenging because agricultural targets are often small, repetitive, occluded, or irregularly shaped, and instructions may refer to one, many, or no objects in an image. Evaluating this capability therefore requires jointly testing localization accuracy, target-set completeness, and existence-aware abstention. To address these challenges, we introduce \textbf{AgroVG}, a multi-source benchmark that formulates agricultural grounding as generalized set prediction: given an image and a referring expression, a model must return all matching target instances or abstain when no target is present. AgroVG contains 10{,}071 annotation-grounded image-query pairs from ten source datasets across six target families: crop/weed, fruit, wheat head, pest, plant disease, and tree canopy. It supports bounding-box grounding (T1) across all six families and instance-mask grounding (T2) on sources with reliable instance-level pixel annotations, with queries covering single-target, multi-target, and target-absent regimes. AgroVG further provides task-specific protocols for box-set matching and query-level mask coverage. Zero-shot evaluation of 26 model configurations spanning closed-source MLLMs, open-source VLMs, and specialized grounding systems reveals persistent gaps: the best multi-target Set-$F_1$ reaches only 0.35, and the best positive-query mask success rate at IoU@0.75 remains below 0.17. Data and code are available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/AgroVG-5172/ .

AI.tldr: AgroVG is a multi-source benchmark for evaluating visual grounding in agriculture, covering 10K+ query-image pairs across 6 target families with bounding-box and instance-mask tasks under single/multi/zero-target regimes, revealing that even the best models achieve only 0.35 Set-F1 and 0.17 mask success rate.
AI.motivation: Agriculture AI needs visual grounding for tasks like weeding/disease monitoring, but existing benchmarks fail to handle small, occluded, repetitive targets and the need for multi-target localization and abstention when no target exists.
AI.method: Formulates agricultural grounding as generalized set prediction; creates 10,071 image-query pairs from 10 datasets across 6 target families; supports bounding-box (T1) and instance-mask (T2) grounding with single/multi/zero-target queries; provides box-set matching and mask coverage evaluation protocols.
AI.result: Evaluated 26 model configurations (MLLMs, VLMs, specialized systems); best multi-target Set-F1 = 0.35; best positive-query mask success at IoU@0.75 < 0.17, showing large performance gaps.
AI.conclusion: Agricultural visual grounding remains extremely challenging, especially for multi-target and high-resolution mask prediction; AgroVG provides a standardized benchmark to drive progress in this domain.

title: HyLoVQA: Dynamic Hypernetwork-Generated Low-Rank Adaptation for Continual Visual Question Answering
summary: Continual Visual Question Answering (VQA) requires learning from non-stationary streams of visual inputs and questions while preserving past knowledge. Most prior methods adapt by updating a largely shared parameter set. This often leads to cross-level task interference, hindering accurate adaptation to the current task and object. To address this limitation, we propose HyLoVQA. It maintains a drift-resilient memory bank of anchors. The bank stores the content of visual objects and textual tasks, and they are updated using current input features. Conditioned on retrieved anchors, a hypernetwork generates lightweight Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) adapters. This ensures parameter efficiency, allowing the model to adapt to each task and object dynamically. Additionally, we formulate an alignment loss that aligns semantic discrepancies in the feature space with functional changes in the parameter space, thereby constraining LoRA adapters to remain focused on the current task and object. Extensive experiments on VQA v2 and NExT-QA under both standard and compositional settings demonstrate the superiority of HyLoVQA over prior state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: HyLoVQA addresses continual VQA by using a drift-resilient memory bank of anchors and hypernetwork-generated LoRA adapters to prevent cross-level task interference, achieving SOTA on VQA v2 and NExT-QA.
AI.motivation: Prior continual VQA methods share large parameter sets across tasks, causing cross-level task interference that hinders accurate adaptation to current tasks and objects.
AI.method: 1) Drift-resilient memory bank storing visual object content and textual task anchors; 2) Hypernetwork generates lightweight LoRA adapters conditioned on retrieved anchors; 3) Alignment loss that connects semantic feature space discrepancies with functional parameter space changes to keep LoRA adapters focused.
AI.result: Outperforms prior SOTA methods on VQA v2 and NExT-QA datasets under both standard and compositional continual learning settings.
AI.conclusion: HyLoVQA effectively mitigates cross-level task interference through dynamic, parameter-efficient adaptation with anchored memory, advancing continual VQA performance.

title: GA-VLN: Geometry-Aware BEV Representation for Efficient Vision-Language Navigation
summary: Despite significant progress in Vision-Language Navigation (VLN), existing approaches still rely on dense RGB videos that produce excessive patch tokens and lack explicit spatial structure, resulting in substantial computational overhead and limited spatial reasoning. To address these issues, we introduce the Geometry-Aware BEV (GA-BEV) - a compact, 3D-grounded feature representation that integrates both explicit and implicit geometric cues into multimodal large language model (MLLM) - based navigation systems. We construct BEV spatial maps from RGB-D inputs by projecting visual features into 3D space and aggregating them into an agent-centric layout that preserves geometric consistency while reducing token redundancy. To further enrich geometric understanding, we incorporate features from a pretrained 3D foundation model into the BEV space, injecting structural priors learned from large-scale 3D reconstruction tasks. Together, these complementary cues - explicit depth-based projection and implicit learned priors - yield compact yet spatially expressive representations that substantially improve navigation efficiency and performance. Experiments show that our method achieves state-of-the-art results using only navigation data, without DAgger augmentation or mixed VQA training, demonstrating the robustness and data efficiency of the proposed GA-VLN framework.

AI.tldr: GA-BEV introduces a geometry-aware BEV representation that combines explicit depth-based projection and implicit 3D priors to reduce token redundancy and improve spatial reasoning in Vision-Language Navigation, achieving SOTA results without extra training data.
AI.motivation: Existing VLN methods rely on dense RGB videos with excessive patch tokens and lack explicit spatial structure, causing high computational cost and limited spatial reasoning ability.
AI.method: Constructs BEV spatial maps from RGB-D inputs by projecting visual features into 3D space and aggregating them into an agent-centric layout; also incorporates features from a pretrained 3D foundation model to inject structural priors, combining explicit depth-based projection with implicit learned priors.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art results using only navigation data, without DAgger augmentation or mixed VQA training, demonstrating robustness and data efficiency.
AI.conclusion: The compact yet spatially expressive GA-BEV representation substantially improves navigation efficiency and performance over prior methods.

title: Physiology and Anatomy Aware Inverse Inference of Myocardial Infarction for Cardiac Digital Twin
comment: Early-accepted by MICCAI 2026. This version corresponds to the submitted version. The final version will be available on Springer Link
summary: Accurate localization of myocardial infarction is essential for risk stratification. While LGE-MRI remains the gold standard, it is resource-intensive. Integrating cine MRI with ECG enables a more detailed representation of infarct properties. Existing inverse MI inference methods overlook realistic scar morphology and cardiac repolarization, reducing sensitivity to subtle ECG variations and interpretability of infarct-induced electrophysiological changes. In this paper, we propose a novel framework for noninvasive MI localization using cardiac digital twins. To bridge the domain gap between simulation and reality, we introduce an anatomy-aware stochastic infarct synthesis strategy to synthesize realistic, irregular scars with border zones, mimicking ischemic transmural progression. We then construct a virtual cohort to simulate QRS-T waveforms, capturing both depolarization and repolarization dynamics. Furthermore, we design a Physiology and Anatomy Aware Network (PAA-Net) that jointly encodes 3D myocardial geometry and multi-lead ECGs to infer infarct areas with varying localizations, sizes, spatial extents, and transmuralities. Experimental results demonstrate that our framework significantly outperforms existing methods in inverse inference, achieving Dice scores of 0.7391 and 0.5503 for scar and border zone segmentation, respectively, while further enhancing the interpretability of the ECG-infarct relationship. Our code will be released upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: A cardiac digital twin framework using anatomy-aware stochastic infarct synthesis and a Physiology & Anatomy Aware Network (PAA-Net) to noninvasively localize myocardial infarction from cine MRI and ECG, achieving superior Dice scores (0.7391 scar, 0.5503 border zone) over existing methods.
AI.motivation: Current inverse MI inference methods ignore realistic scar morphology (irregular shape, border zones) and cardiac repolarization, leading to low sensitivity to subtle ECG changes and poor interpretability. LGE-MRI is the gold standard but resource-intensive, motivating a noninvasive, integrative approach using cine MRI and ECG.
AI.method: 1) Anatomy-aware stochastic infarct synthesis generates realistic irregular scars with border zones mimicking transmural progression. 2) Virtual cohort construction simulates QRS-T waveforms capturing both depolarization and repolarization. 3) Physiology and Anatomy Aware Network (PAA-Net) jointly encodes 3D myocardial geometry and multi-lead ECGs to infer infarct location, size, extent, and transmurality.
AI.result: Outperforms existing inverse inference methods with Dice scores of 0.7391 for scar and 0.5503 for border zone segmentation, with improved interpretability of ECG-infarct relationships.
AI.conclusion: The proposed digital twin framework enables accurate, noninvasive MI localization by bridging simulation-reality gaps and enhancing interpretability of electrophysiological changes induced by infarction.

title: Broken Memories: Detecting and Mitigating Memorization in Diffusion Models with Degraded Generations
summary: While diffusion models excel at generating high-quality images, their tendency to memorize training data poses significant privacy and copyright risks. In this work, we for the first time identify that memorization induces internal numerical instability, often manifesting as visually ``broken'' artifacts. Inspired by stability analysis in numerical methods, we introduce empirical stability regions based on latent update norms to quantitatively characterize stable behavior during generation. Leveraging this, we propose a principled, on-the-fly framework for step-wise detection and adaptive mitigation. Our approach suppresses memorization without altering prompts or guidance, thereby preserving semantic fidelity and image quality. Extensive experiments on Stable Diffusion 1.4 demonstrate that our method achieves an AUC $>0.999$ detection performance and a $0.0\%$ memorization rate after mitigation with negligible overhead ($\approx0.01$s per image).

AI.tldr: This paper identifies memorization in diffusion models as internal numerical instability causing "broken" artifacts, proposes a detection/mitigation framework using empirical stability regions, achieving >0.999 AUC detection and 0% memorization with negligible overhead in Stable Diffusion 1.4.
AI.motivation: To address privacy and copyright risks from memorization in diffusion models, which generate high-quality images but tend to memorize training data.
AI.method: Introduces empirical stability regions based on latent update norms to characterize stable generation behavior, then develops an on-the-fly framework for step-wise memorization detection and adaptive mitigation without altering prompts or guidance.
AI.result: Achieves AUC >0.999 for memorization detection and 0.0% memorization rate after mitigation on Stable Diffusion 1.4, with minimal overhead (~0.01s per image).
AI.conclusion: Memorization in diffusion models can be effectively detected via numerical instability and adaptively mitigated while preserving semantic fidelity and image quality.

title: EasyVFX: Frequency-Driven Decoupling for Resource-Efficient VFX Generation
comment: Accepted by SIGGRAPH 2026. Project page: https://easy-vfx.github.io/
summary: Generating high-fidelity visual effects (VFX) typically demands massive datasets and prohibitive computational power due to the intricate coupling of spatial textures and temporal dynamics. In this paper, we introduce EasyVFX, a resource-efficient framework that achieves realistic VFX synthesis under stringent constraints. Our core philosophy lies in frequency-domain decomposition: we observe that the complexity of VFX can be significantly mitigated by decoupling high-frequency components, which represent intricate spatial appearances, from low-frequency components that encapsulate global motion dynamics. This spectral disentanglement transforms a high-dimensional learning problem into manageable sub-tasks, thereby lowering the optimization barrier and reducing data dependency. Building upon this insight, we propose a two-stage training paradigm. First, we design a Frequency-aware Mixture-of-Experts (Freq-MoE) architecture. By utilizing a soft routing mechanism, our model assigns specialized experts to distinct spectral bands, enabling them to cultivate robust priors for appearance and motion dynamics. This specialization allows the model to acquire foundational VFX knowledge with fewer GPU resources. Second, we introduce a Test-Time Training strategy powered by a novel Frequency-constraint Loss. This allows the pre-trained model to swiftly adapt to specific, unseen effects through localized optimizations, requiring only about 100 steps on a single GPU. Experimental results demonstrate that EasyVFX produces structurally consistent and visually stunning effects, proving that frequency-aware learning is a key catalyst for democratizing professional-grade VFX.

AI.tldr: EasyVFX is a resource-efficient framework for realistic VFX synthesis that decomposes visual effects into high-frequency (appearance) and low-frequency (motion) components via frequency-domain decomposition, using a Frequency-aware Mixture-of-Experts (Freq-MoE) architecture and Test-Time Training with Frequency-constraint Loss to achieve professional-grade VFX with minimal GPU resources.
AI.motivation: High-fidelity VFX synthesis traditionally requires massive datasets and prohibitive computational power due to the complex coupling of spatial textures and temporal dynamics. The authors aim to democratize professional-grade VFX by reducing resource requirements.
AI.method: 1) Frequency-domain decomposition decouples high-frequency (intricate spatial appearances) from low-frequency (global motion dynamics) components. 2) Frequency-aware Mixture-of-Experts (Freq-MoE) with soft routing assigns specialized experts to distinct spectral bands. 3) Test-Time Training strategy with Frequency-constraint Loss enables rapid adaptation (~100 steps on single GPU).
AI.result: EasyVFX produces structurally consistent and visually stunning VFX effects while requiring significantly fewer GPU resources and less data than traditional approaches.
AI.conclusion: Frequency-aware learning is a key catalyst for democratizing professional-grade VFX, enabling resource-constrained scenarios to achieve realistic synthesis.

title: Distributed Image Compression with Multimodal Side Information at Extremely Low Bitrates
summary: Distributed Image Compression (DIC) is crucial for multi-view transmission, especially when operating at extremely low bitrates (< 0.1 bpp). Its core challenge is effectively utilizing side information to achieve high-quality reconstruction under strict bitrate budgets. However, existing DIC approaches struggle to exploit global context and object-level details from side information, leading to local blurring and the loss of fine details in the reconstruction. To address these limitations, we propose a Multimodal DIC framework (MDIC), which, for the first time, leverages side information in a multimodal manner into the DIC paradigm, effectively preserving fine-grained local details and enhancing global perceptual quality in reconstructed images. Specifically, we introduce a text-to-image diffusion-based decoder conditioned on textual side information extracted from correlated images to capture shared global semantics. Moreover, we design a feature-mask generator, supervised by a multimodal fine-grained alignment task, to strengthen the exploitation of visual side information. The generated mask serves two purposes: first, it guides the extraction of fine-grained details from losslessly transmitted side information to preserve the semantic consistency of reconstructed details; second, it regulates the extraction of clustered feature representations from the quantized VQ-VAE embeddings, compensating for category information lost under the extreme compression of the primary image. Extensive experiments on the widely used KITTI Stereo and Cityscapes datasets demonstrate that MDIC achieves state-of-the-art perceptual quality at extremely low bitrates.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes MDIC, a Multimodal Distributed Image Compression framework that leverages both text and visual side information via diffusion models and feature-mask generators to achieve state-of-the-art perceptual quality at extremely low bitrates (< 0.1 bpp).
AI.motivation: Existing DIC methods fail to exploit global context and object-level details from side information, resulting in local blurring and loss of fine details during reconstruction at extremely low bitrates.
AI.method: (1) A text-to-image diffusion-based decoder conditioned on textual side information from correlated images; (2) A feature-mask generator supervised by multimodal fine-grained alignment, which guides detail extraction from side information and regulates VQ-VAE embeddings to compensate for lost category information.
AI.result: State-of-the-art perceptual quality on KITTI Stereo and Cityscapes datasets at extremely low bitrates (< 0.1 bpp).
AI.conclusion: Multimodal side information (text + visual) significantly improves DIC performance at ultra-low bitrates, with the diffusion-based decoder and feature-mask generator being key enablers.

title: Echo4DIR: 4D Implicit Heart Reconstruction from 2D Echocardiography Videos
summary: Reconstructing 4D (3D+t) cardiac geometry from sparse 2D echocardiography is highly desirable yet fundamentally challenged by geometric ambiguity and temporal discontinuity. To tackle these issues, we propose Echo4DIR, a novel test-time 4D implicit reconstruction framework. Specifically, we learn robust 3D shape priors from statistical shape models (SSMs) via a cardiac conditional SDF, constructing an Epipolar Mask Encoder module with epipolar cross attention to effectively fuse multi-view features. To bridge the synthetic-to-real domain gap, we introduce a self-supervised SDF-tailored differentiable rendering strategy for patient-specific 3D shape adaptation using uncalibrated clinical masks without requiring 3D ground truth. Crucially, the inherent continuity of implicit representation overcomes sparse observations, enabling anatomically reliable geometry at arbitrary resolutions. Furthermore, to empower our framework with physically continuous 4D extension, we introduce a Radial SDF Alignment strategy that strictly locks shape evolution to the predicted velocity field, fundamentally eliminating mesh drift. Extensive experiments on synthetic benchmarks and real clinical datasets demonstrate that Echo4DIR achieves state-of-the-art 4D cardiac mesh reconstruction, notably yielding an impressive clinical overlap of up to 98.35% Dice and 96.75% IoU.

AI.tldr: Echo4DIR is a test-time 4D implicit reconstruction framework that reconstructs continuous 4D cardiac geometry from sparse 2D echocardiography using statistical shape models, epipolar cross-attention, self-supervised differentiable rendering, and Radial SDF Alignment, achieving state-of-the-art results (98.35% Dice, 96.75% IoU).
AI.motivation: Reconstructing 4D cardiac geometry from sparse 2D echocardiography is highly desirable but fundamentally challenged by geometric ambiguity and temporal discontinuity.
AI.method: 1) Learns 3D shape priors from SSMs via cardiac conditional SDF; 2) Epipolar Mask Encoder with cross-attention for multi-view feature fusion; 3) Self-supervised SDF-tailored differentiable rendering for synthetic-to-real domain adaptation; 4) Radial SDF Alignment for physically continuous 4D extension locking shape evolution to predicted velocity field.
AI.result: State-of-the-art 4D cardiac mesh reconstruction with 98.35% Dice and 96.75% IoU on synthetic benchmarks and real clinical datasets.
AI.conclusion: Echo4DIR effectively addresses geometric ambiguity and temporal discontinuity in 4D cardiac reconstruction from sparse 2D echocardiography, demonstrating clinically promising results.

title: COCOTree: A Dataset and Benchmark for Open Tree-Structured Visual Decomposition
summary: We formalize and enable the task of open tree decomposition, which segments an image into hierarchical trees of visual components with unconstrained granularity and flexibility. Specifically, we provide the foundation benchmark for this new paradigm with the following three key contributions. First, we overcome the prohibitively high cognitive and physical bottlenecks of manual annotation by developing a fully automated generation pipeline that synergizes the semantic reasoning of Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) with the precise geometric grounding of SAM 3. Second, leveraging this pipeline, we construct COCOTree, a massive-scale benchmark featuring over 21K images and 1.8M structural nodes. By embracing an open-vocabulary space of over 3.5K unique labels, it successfully captures the long-tail distribution of complex physical assemblies. Notably, rigorous human evaluation confirms our generated annotations demonstrate strong alignment with human structural judgment. Third, we establish a standardized evaluation protocol by proposing the Open Tree Quality (OTQ) metric, which jointly assesses mask precision, label accuracy, and structural consistency. We release our dataset and benchmark code at https://github.com/melonkick3090/COCOTree.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces COCOTree, a large-scale benchmark for open tree decomposition—segmenting images into hierarchical trees of visual components. It uses an automated pipeline combining LVLMs and SAM 3 to generate 21K+ images with 1.8M structural nodes and 3.5K+ unique labels, evaluated via the new Open Tree Quality (OTQ) metric.
AI.motivation: To overcome the cognitive and physical bottlenecks of manual annotation for hierarchical image segmentation, and to formalize the new task of open tree decomposition at scale.
AI.method: A fully automated generation pipeline synergizing Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) for semantic reasoning with SAM 3 for precise geometric grounding, producing hierarchical tree structures of visual components.
AI.result: Constructed COCOTree benchmark with 21K+ images, 1.8M structural nodes, and 3.5K+ unique labels capturing long-tail distributions. Human evaluation confirms strong alignment with human structural judgment.
AI.conclusion: The proposed pipeline and benchmark enable open tree decomposition with unconstrained granularity, and the OTQ metric provides a standardized evaluation protocol jointly assessing mask precision, label accuracy, and structural consistency.

title: TWINGS: Thin Plate Splines Warp-aligned Initialization for Sparse-View Gaussian Splatting
comment: Accepted to CVPR 2025, Project page: https://sandokim.github.io/twings/
summary: Novel view synthesis from sparse-view inputs poses a significant challenge in 3D computer vision, particularly for achieving high-quality scene reconstructions with limited viewpoints. We introduce TWINGS, a framework that enhances 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) by directly addressing point sparsity. We employ Thin Plate Splines (TPS), a smooth non-rigid deformation model that minimizes bending energy to estimate a globally coherent warp from control-point correspondences, to align backprojected points from estimated depth with triangulated 3D control points, yielding calibrated backprojected points. By sampling these calibrated points near the control points, TWINGS provides a fast and geometrically accurate initialization for 3DGS, ultimately improving structural detail preservation and color fidelity in reconstructed scenes. Extensive experiments on DTU, LLFF, and Mip-NeRF360 demonstrate that TWINGS consistently outperforms existing methods, delivering detailed and accurate reconstructions under sparse-view scenarios.

AI.tldr: TWINGS enhances 3D Gaussian Splatting for sparse-view novel view synthesis by using Thin Plate Splines to align backprojected depth points with triangulated control points, providing better initialization and achieving superior reconstruction quality on DTU, LLFF, and Mip-NeRF360 datasets.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of point sparsity in sparse-view 3D scene reconstruction, which limits high-quality novel view synthesis.
AI.method: Uses Thin Plate Splines (TPS) for smooth non-rigid deformation to align backprojected depth points with triangulated 3D control points, producing calibrated points for 3DGS initialization.
AI.result: Outperforms existing methods on DTU, LLFF, and Mip-NeRF360 benchmarks in terms of structural detail and color fidelity under sparse-view conditions.
AI.conclusion: TWINGS provides a fast, geometrically accurate initialization for 3DGS, enabling detailed and accurate sparse-view reconstructions.

title: JMed48k: A Multi-Profession Japanese Medical Licensing Benchmark for Vision-Language Model Evaluation
summary: We introduce JMed48k, a multi-profession Japanese healthcare licensing benchmark for evaluating vision-language models. Built from official PDF materials released by the Japanese Ministry of Health, Labour and Welfare, JMed48k contains 48,862 exam questions and 20,142 images from 11 national licensing examinations between 2005 and 2025, with visual content annotated under an 8-type taxonomy. From this corpus, we derive JMed48k-Eval, a recent five-year evaluation subset with 12,484 scored questions, including 9,905 text-only questions and 2,579 questions with images. We evaluate 21 proprietary, open-source, and medical-specific models, reporting text-only and with-image performance separately. Because these subsets contain different questions, we further introduce a paired image-removal audit that evaluates questions with images before and after removing visual content to explore four answer-transition states. The audit shows that proprietary and open source models gain substantially from images, whereas medical-specific systems show limited observable use of visual evidence, with many correct answers persisting after image removal. Even among proprietary models, the net image-removal effect varies sevenfold across professions, from +5.7 points on Physician questions to +39.8 points on Public Health Nurse questions. We release JMed48k to support reproducible, profession-stratified evaluation of vision-language models in medical licensing settings.

AI.tldr: JMed48k is a large Japanese healthcare licensing benchmark (48,862 questions, 20,142 images from 11 exams, 2005-2025) for evaluating VLMs, with a five-year eval subset (12,484 questions). Evaluation of 21 models shows proprietary/open-source models benefit from images, while medical-specific systems show limited visual utility; image-removal effects vary 7x across professions.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of multi-profession, reproducible, and visually-rich Japanese healthcare licensing benchmarks for evaluating vision-language models in medical domains.
AI.method: Built from official Japanese MHLW PDFs (11 national exams, 2005-2025) with 8-type image taxonomy; derived a recent 5-year subset (JMed48k-Eval); evaluated 21 models with text-only and with-image splits; introduced a paired image-removal audit to analyze answer-transition states.
AI.result: Proprietary and open-source models benefit substantially from images; medical-specific models show limited visual evidence use (many correct answers persist after image removal). Net image-removal effect varies 7x across professions (e.g., +5.7 Physician vs. +39.8 Public Health Nurse).
AI.conclusion: JMed48k enables profession-stratified, reproducible evaluation of VLMs in Japanese medical licensing; reveals significant disparities in visual capability utilization across models and professions.

title: GenHAR: Generalizing Cross-domain Human Activity Recognition for Last-mile Delivery
summary: Human Activity Recognition (HAR) has shown remarkable effectiveness in various applications, such as smart healthcare and intelligent manufacturing. However, a major challenge faced by HAR is the distribution shift across different sensor data domains, which often leads to decreased performance when deployed for real-world applications. To address this issue, this paper introduces GenHAR, a novel framework designed to mitigate the domain gap by learning domain-invariant sensor representations. GenHAR aims to enhance the generalization capabilities of HAR on target domains purely with data from the source domain. The key novelty of GenHAR lies in two aspects. Firstly, GenHAR tokenizes sensor data and learns correlations among frequency sensor channel dimensions to improve the robustness of HAR models. Secondly, GenHAR improves the efficiency via selective masking and an efficient attention mechanism. We conduct a systematic analysis of GenHAR by comparing it with state-of-the-art HAR methods on real-world human activity datasets. Results show that GenHAR outperforms state-of-the-art methods by 9.97% in accuracy, and reduces Floating Point Operations by 6.4 times. Moreover, we deploy GenHAR at a leading logistics company in 4 cities, and have detected 2.15 billion real-time activities. We release our code at: https://github.com/Sensor-FoundationModel/GenHAR.

AI.tldr: GenHAR is a novel framework for Human Activity Recognition that mitigates domain shift across sensor data by learning domain-invariant representations through sensor tokenization, frequency channel correlation learning, selective masking, and efficient attention, achieving 9.97% higher accuracy and 6.4x fewer FLOPs than SOTA, deployed in 4 cities detecting 2.15B activities.
AI.motivation: HAR faces performance degradation in real-world deployment due to distribution shift across different sensor data domains, which limits generalization.
AI.method: GenHAR tokenizes sensor data, learns correlations among frequency sensor channel dimensions, and improves efficiency via selective masking and an efficient attention mechanism to learn domain-invariant representations.
AI.result: Outperforms SOTA methods by 9.97% in accuracy, reduces FLOPs by 6.4x, deployed at a leading logistics company in 4 cities detecting 2.15 billion real-time activities.
AI.conclusion: GenHAR effectively addresses domain shift in HAR, achieving superior accuracy and efficiency, with demonstrated real-world scalability.

title: LVDrive: Latent Visual Representation Enhanced Vision-Language-Action Autonomous Driving Model
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have emerged as a promising framework for end-to-end autonomous driving. However, existing VLAs typically rely on sparse action supervision, which underutilizes their powerful scene understanding and reasoning capabilities. Recent attempts to incorporate dense visual supervision via world modeling often overemphasize pixel-level image reconstruction, neglecting semantically meaningful scene representation learning. In this work, we propose LVDrive, a Latent Visual representation enhanced VLA framework for autonomous driving. LVDrive introduces a future scene prediction task into the VLA paradigm, where future representations are learned entirely in a high-level latent space under auxiliary supervision from a pretrained vision backbone. Departing from inefficient autoregressive generation, we jointly model future scene and motion prediction within a unified embedding space, processed in a single forward pass to conduct the future-aware reasoning. We further design a two-stage trajectory decoding strategy that explicitly leverages the learned latent future representations to refine trajectory generation. Extensive experiments on the challenging Bench2Drive benchmark demonstrate that LVDrive achieves significant improvements in closed-loop driving performance, outperforming both action supervised methods and image-reconstruction-based world model approaches.

AI.tldr: LVDrive enhances VLA models for autonomous driving by adding future scene prediction in latent space, replacing inefficient pixel-level reconstruction, and using a two-stage trajectory decoder, achieving superior closed-loop driving performance.
AI.motivation: Existing VLA models underutilize their scene understanding due to sparse action supervision, and world model approaches overemphasize pixel-level reconstruction instead of learning semantically meaningful representations.
AI.method: LVDrive introduces (1) future scene prediction in high-level latent space using a pretrained vision backbone, (2) joint modeling of future scene and motion in a unified embedding with single-forward-pass processing, and (3) a two-stage trajectory decoding strategy that leverages latent future representations for trajectory refinement.
AI.result: On the Bench2Drive benchmark, LVDrive significantly outperforms both action-supervised methods and image-reconstruction-based world model approaches in closed-loop driving.
AI.conclusion: Learning future-aware latent representations is more effective than pixel-level reconstruction for improving VLA-based autonomous driving, enabling better reasoning and trajectory generation.

title: VISTA: Validation-Guided Integration of Spatial and Temporal Foundation Models with Anatomical Decoding for Rare-Pathology VCE Event Detection -- after competition results
summary: Capsule endoscopy event detection is challenging because clinically relevant findings are sparse, visually heterogeneous, and evaluated at the event level rather than by frame accuracy. We propose VISTA, a metric-aligned multi-backbone framework for the RAREVISION task. VISTA combines EndoFM-LV for temporal context and DINOv3 ViTL/16 for frame-level visual semantics, followed by a Diverse Head Ensemble (DHE), Validation-Guided Weighted Fusion (VGWF), and Anatomy-Aware Temporal Event Decoding (ATED). The original official submission achieved hidden-test temporal mAP@0.5 of 0.3530 and mAP@0.95 of 0.3235. After the competition, extending local threshold refinement with a global coarse search improved performance to 0.3726 mAP@0.5 and 0.3431 mAP@0.95, ranking Team ACVLab second in the post-competition evaluation.

AI.tldr: VISTA is a multi-backbone framework combining EndoFM-LV and DINOv3 with ensemble and fusion techniques for capsule endoscopy event detection, achieving second place in the RAREVISION task.
AI.motivation: Capsule endoscopy event detection is challenging due to sparse, visually heterogeneous findings evaluated at the event level rather than frame accuracy, motivating a metric-aligned approach.
AI.method: VISTA combines EndoFM-LV (temporal context) and DINOv3 ViTL/16 (frame-level semantics), plus Diverse Head Ensemble (DHE), Validation-Guided Weighted Fusion (VGWF), and Anatomy-Aware Temporal Event Decoding (ATED). Post-competition refinement used global coarse search with local threshold refinement.
AI.result: Original submission achieved hidden-test temporal mAP@0.5 of 0.3530 and mAP@0.95 of 0.3235. Post-competition improved to 0.3726 mAP@0.5 and 0.3431 mAP@0.95, ranking second.
AI.conclusion: The proposed metric-aligned framework with multi-backbone design and post-hoc refinement effectively addresses event-level detection in capsule endoscopy.

title: TextTeacher: What Can Language Teach About Images?
summary:
The platonic representation hypothesis suggests that sufficiently large models converge to a shared representation geometry, even across modalities.
